scope.summary: F-LOW DB4: four INTERVAL DAY TO SECOND serializers interpolated individually-signed i32 components, so a negative interval rendered with embedded minus signs; one checked formatter now owns the sign for every path.
out_of_scope: INTERVAL YEAR TO MONTH, which formats two components and is not part of this finding.
The typed numeric fields in the structured and OSON payloads: they keep their original signed values, as the bead requires. Only the rendered text is canonicalized.
The driver's own Arrow/OSON rendering in oracledb 0.8.4, which has the same shape upstream; this fixes the server's serializers only.

entry_points.name: format_interval_ds / format_interval_ds_iso / interval_ds_mixed_sign_marker
entry_points.trace: 464 oraclemcp-db lib tests pass. Negative acceptance: a wholly negative interval renders '-1 02:03:04.000000005' and '-P1DT2H3M4.000000005S', each containing exactly one '-'; the sub-day case (0,0,0,0,-1) renders '-0 00:00:00.000000001' with no sign after the first character. Positive acceptance: positive and zero intervals are asserted byte-identical to the previous format string in both the direct and ISO forms. Mixed signs return None and the structured paths emit a typed unsupported marker with value=null and the original signed fields preserved. i32::MIN renders '-2147483648 00:00:00.000000000' via unsigned_abs rather than overflowing.

limitations: Discriminating, but the first mutation attempt silently no-op'd on an indentation mismatch and left the suite green — which would have recorded a false 'discriminating' result. Re-run with an assertion that the replacement landed, removing the leading sign fails 3 of the 5 tests.
Unit-level: no live Oracle session produced a negative INTERVAL DS during this work, so the fixtures are constructed values rather than driver-decoded ones.
Scoped verification: cargo test -p oraclemcp-db --lib and clippy over the touched file. No workspace-wide run.
